task: I’m trying to get a small vendor setup approved without bouncing between departments forever. Can you help me map the usual steps and what paperwork I should have ready so I don’t get stuck waiting on avoidable back-and-forth?

turns.agent_response:
Yes — the fastest way to get a vendor setup through is to treat it like a **handoff problem**, not a paperwork problem.

Here’s the usual flow and what to have ready at each step so you don’t get stalled.

| Step | What usually happens | What they’ll ask for | What to have ready |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1. Vendor request / intake | Someone opens the request in procurement/AP | Legal name, address, tax ID, contact info | W-9/W-8, legal entity name, remittance address, primary contact |
| 2. Compliance review | They check if the vendor is allowed to do business | Insurance, certifications, sanctions/COI checks | COI, licenses, certifications, diversity docs if relevant |
| 3. Risk / security review | IT, security, or info governance reviews access/data risk | Will they touch data, systems, or site access? | Security questionnaire, data handling summary, NDA if needed |
| 4. Contract review | Legal/procurement reviews terms | MSA, SOW, pricing, payment terms | Clean draft agreement, scope, fee schedule, term, termination, liability limits |
| 5. Finance setup | AP creates the vendor in the system | Banking, tax, invoice instructions | ACH form, bank letter if required, remittance instructions, invoice format |
| 6. Approval routing | Internal approvers sign off | Budget owner, department head, legal, finance | Business justification, cost center, approver names, urgency reason |
| 7. Activation | Vendor gets entered and marked ready to pay | Final confirmations | Signed docs, approved request number, vendor ID if assigned |

### The paperwork bundle to prep upfront
If you want to avoid the usual ping-pong, send a single packet with:

- **Vendor legal name**
- **Tax form**: W-9 or W-8
- **ACH / banking info**
- **Remittance address**
- **Primary contact + backup contact**
- **Scope of work**
- **Pricing / rate sheet**
- **Insurance certificate** if required
- **Any licenses / certifications**
- **Signed NDA** if needed
- **Security questionnaire** if they’ll access systems/data
- **Invoice instructions**: who to bill, PO required or not, net terms
- **Business justification**: why this vendor, why now
- **Approver / sponsor name** inside the company

### Common reasons it gets stuck
- Legal name on the W-9 doesn’t match the contract
- Banking info is missing or not in the expected format
- No clear internal sponsor
- Scope is vague, so procurement keeps asking for clarification
- They need insurance or security docs you didn’t know about
- The contract has payment terms or liability language they won’t accept
- No one knows which department owns the approval

### Best way to avoid back-and-forth
1. **Ask who owns the workflow first** — procurement, AP, legal, or the business owner.
2. **Send the full packet in one email** with a short cover note.
3. **Use their exact vendor form names** if they have them.
4. **Include one internal sponsor** who can answer questions fast.
5. **Pre-answer the obvious blockers**: tax, banking, insurance, scope, payment terms.

### Short cover note you can use
> Attached is the full vendor setup packet for approval. Included are the W-9, banking details, scope, pricing, insurance, and primary contacts. Please let me know if any department needs additional documentation so we can resolve everything in one pass.

If you want, I can turn this into a **one-page vendor setup checklist** you can reuse every time.

user_simulator.text:
Pretty good, but I want it tighter and more practical. The table is fine. What I need next is the actual **one-pass checklist** I can hand to someone without them freelancing extra questions.

Also split it by **must-have vs. only-if-applicable**. In real life that’s where the churn happens. And if there are common blockers for small vendors specifically — no insurance, no ACH, sole prop vs LLC, no PO number, whatever — put those in. That’s the useful part.

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — here’s the tighter **one-pass vendor setup checklist** version.

## Vendor setup checklist

### Must-have
| Item | What to provide | Why it matters |
|---|---|---|
| Legal vendor name | Exact legal entity name | Must match tax and contract docs |
| Tax form | W-9 or W-8 | Required for AP / tax setup |
| Mailing / remittance address | Where checks or notices go | Prevents payment setup delays |
| Primary contact | Name, email, phone | Someone has to answer follow-up questions |
| Internal sponsor | Business owner / requester | Keeps the request from drifting |
| Scope of work | What the vendor is doing | Needed for approval and contract review |
| Pricing | Fixed fee, hourly rate, or rate sheet | Finance/procurement need the number |
| Payment terms | Net 15 / 30 / 45, milestone, etc. | AP and contract review |
| Invoice instructions | PO required or not, billing contact | Avoids rejected invoices |
| Business justification | Why this vendor, why now | Helps approval routing |

### Only if applicable
| Item | When needed | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| ACH / banking info | If they pay by direct deposit | Small vendors often miss this |
| Insurance certificate | If required by policy or contract | Common blocker if they don’t carry it |
| NDA | If any confidential info is shared | Often needed before details are exchanged |
| Security questionnaire | If vendor touches systems/data | Can stall the process if not flagged early |
| Licenses / certifications | Regulated or specialized work | Must match the work being done |
| Diversity / supplier docs | If your company tracks this | Usually optional but requested later |
| Signed contract / MSA / SOW | If legal review is part of setup | Best to send clean draft upfront |
| PO number | If your company requires one | Some AP teams won’t activate without it |
| Vendor insurance addendum | If your company has specific coverage rules | Often overlooked until late |

## Common blockers for small vendors
- **No ACH info** and they only expect paper checks
- **Sole prop vs. LLC mismatch** between tax form and contract
- **No insurance** when the company requires it
- **No PO number** when AP won’t process without one
- **Legal name mismatch** across W-9, invoice, and contract
- **Scope too vague** so procurement keeps asking for clarification
- **No internal sponsor** to push it through
- **Missing invoice format** so first invoice gets kicked back
- **Banking details not in the expected format** or sent too late
- **Security review triggered late** after someone already promised access

## One-pass rule
If you want to avoid churn, send **one packet** that includes:
- vendor legal name
- tax form
- contact info
- remittance info
- scope
- pricing
- payment terms
- invoice instructions
- sponsor name
- applicable docs only

## Short cover note
> Attached is the complete vendor setup packet. It includes the tax form, legal name, contact details, scope, pricing, payment terms, and any applicable supporting documents. Please let me know in one reply if anything else is required so we can avoid multiple rounds.

If you want, I can turn this into a **fillable intake form** you can reuse internally.

user_simulator.text:
Better. Still a little generic.

What I need is the version that reads like an intake sheet, not a memo. Plain labels. No fluff. Something I can drop into email or an internal form and force people to answer once.

Also add a hard distinction for:
- **required to start setup**
- **required before first payment**

That split matters. A lot of these places will let a vendor sit in limbo because finance and procurement are hiding behind each other.

And on the small-vendor blockers, call out the ugly ones more directly:
- sole prop with no EIN
- vendor only takes paper checks
- no insurance / underinsured
- no W-9 yet
- no contract, just verbal agreement
- no PO process at all
- bank account in personal name instead of business name

That’s the part people actually trip over.

So yeah — give me the actual one-pass checklist form, tightened up, with those two timing buckets.

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — here’s the **one-pass vendor intake checklist** in a plain form style, split by:

- **Required to start setup**
- **Required before first payment**
- **Common small-vendor blockers**

You can paste this into email or an internal form as-is.

---

# Vendor Setup Intake Checklist

## Required to start setup
**Vendor legal name:**  
**DBA / trade name:**  
**Entity type:** sole prop / LLC / corporation / partnership / nonprofit / other  
**Tax ID:** EIN / SSN / foreign tax ID  
**Tax form:** W-9 / W-8 / not yet available  
**Business address:**  
**Remit address:**  
**Primary contact name:**  
**Primary contact email:**  
**Primary contact phone:**  
**Internal requester / sponsor:**  
**Department / cost center:**  
**What is being purchased:**  
**Scope / deliverable:**  
**Estimated amount:**  
**Pricing type:** fixed fee / hourly / rate card / milestone / other  
**Start date needed:**  
**Is a contract required?** yes / no / unknown  
**Is a PO required?** yes / no / unknown  
**Will vendor access company data, systems, or site?** yes / no  
**Will vendor receive confidential information?** yes / no  
**Any required approvals known already:** procurement / legal / finance / security / insurance / other

---

## Required before first payment
**Signed contract / MSA / SOW:** yes / no  
**Approved vendor record / vendor ID:** yes / no  
**PO number:** yes / no / not required  
**ACH / banking info:** yes / no / not available  
**Bank account name:** business / personal / other  
**Invoice instructions provided:** yes / no  
**Invoice contact / billing email:**  
**Payment terms agreed:** net 15 / net 30 / milestone / other  
**Insurance certificate on file:** yes / no / not required  
**Required licenses / certifications on file:** yes / no / not required  
**NDA signed:** yes / no / not required  
**Security review complete:** yes / no / not required  
**Any required tax or compliance docs complete:** yes / no

---

## Common small-vendor blockers
Check any that apply:

- **Sole prop with no EIN**
- **No W-9 yet**
- **Vendor only takes paper checks**
- **No ACH available**
- **Bank account is in personal name, not business name**
- **No insurance**
- **Underinsured**
- **No contract, only verbal agreement**
- **No PO process at all**
- **No internal sponsor**
- **Legal name does not match W-9 / invoice / contract**
- **Scope is vague**
- **Invoice format not agreed**
- **Security review triggered late**
- **Vendor wants to start before paperwork is done**

---

## One-line rule for routing
**Do not start setup until the “Required to start setup” section is complete.**  
**Do not release payment until the “Required before first payment” section is complete.**

---

## Short email version
> Please complete the vendor intake below in one reply.  
>  
> **Required to start setup:** legal name, entity type, tax ID, W-9/W-8, address, contacts, requester, cost center, scope, amount, pricing type, start date, contract/PO status, data access/confidentiality, known approvals.  
>  
> **Required before first payment:** signed contract, vendor ID, PO number if required, ACH/banking, invoice instructions, payment terms, insurance, licenses/certifications, NDA, security review, tax/compliance docs.  
>  
> Also note any blockers such as no EIN, no W-9, paper-check only, no ACH, personal bank account, no insurance, no contract, no PO process, or mismatched legal name.

If you want, I can turn this into a **fillable form layout** with yes/no checkboxes and blanks, ready to copy into Word or Google Forms.

user_simulator.text:
Closer. Still a little too tidy. I need it in a form layout, not a writeup about the form.

Give me:
- plain field labels
- yes/no checkboxes where it fits
- a hard split between **start setup** and **first payment**
- a separate **blockers** section

And cut the explanatory lines. I don’t need the little rule paragraph or the offer to turn it into something else. Just the intake sheet I can paste straight into an email or internal form.
